methodology: Derived from published academic research on earnings call language and stock price reactions. Key sources: Loughran & McDonald (2011, 2016) on financial text sentiment and market impact; Tetlock (2007) on media sentiment and trading; Price et al. (2012) on earnings call tone and abnormal returns; Brockman et al. (2015) on managerial optimism in conference calls; Huang et al. (2014) on analyst tone and forecast accuracy. Score bins calibrated to 0-100 scale from NLP-based analysis matching our engine's scoring dimensions.
disclaimer: Historical patterns only. Not financial advice. Past language-price correlations do not guarantee future outcomes.

0-9.label: Very Negative
0-9.sample_context: Extremely negative tone, high hedging, multiple red flags

10-19.label: Strongly Negative
10-19.sample_context: Very negative sentiment with low confidence language

20-29.label: Negative
20-29.sample_context: Predominantly negative with hedging and deflection

30-39.label: Moderately Negative
30-39.sample_context: Mixed signals leaning negative, some uncertainty

40-49.label: Slightly Negative
40-49.sample_context: Below-average tone with some positive elements

70-79.label: Positive
70-79.sample_context: Strong positive tone with executive conviction

80-89.label: Strongly Positive
80-89.sample_context: Very positive with high clarity and ownership

sentiment.description: Positive/negative word ratios most correlated with next-day returns (Loughran & McDonald 2011)

confidence.description: Hedging language and certainty markers predict analyst reception (Brockman et al. 2015)

clarity.description: Readability and specificity reduce information asymmetry (Li 2008)

red_flags.description: Evasive patterns and topic avoidance signal negative information withholding (Hollander et al. 2010)

ownership.description: Executive accountability language builds analyst confidence (Price et al. 2012)
